cockroachdb/cockroach#20296.body:
**FEATURE REQUEST**

One big plus for trying cockroachdb was its compatibilty with postgres. I assumed pg_dump would work, instead it errors out.

```
$ pg_dump -h db1 -p 26257 database
pg_dump: [archiver (db)] query failed: ERROR:  unknown function: pg_catalog.pg_is_in_recovery()
pg_dump: [archiver (db)] query was: SELECT pg_catalog.pg_is_in_recovery()
```

I've also tried 'cockroachdb dump > out.sql' but the SQL is not postgres compatible. `id STRING(128)` for example. I hand edited the schema to try and get this route to work, but loading the data itself hits schema constraints since a foreign key field means table B must come before table A, but the sql dump file loads table A then B. pg_restore is supposed to have a work-around for this, which is why I'd like to have pg_dump work. 

I belive pg_is_in_recovery is relatively new for postgres, so perhaps this only recently stopped working.


Jira issue: CRDB-5936

Epic CRDB-28751
cockroachdb/cockroach#20296.title: sql: support pg_dump

cockroachdb/cockroach#79212.body:
Admission control is currently opt-in, in the sense that it's controlled by `BatchRequest.AdmissionHeader` and the zero value for `Source` (`OTHER`) is currently excluded from admission control. I believe all SQL traffic sets this header, but lots of internal traffic does not, which can lead to stores getting overloaded (in particular with bulk operations).

For example, the widely used `DB.Txn()` and `kv.NewTxn()` functions do not set admission control headers (nor do any of the other `DB` methods). In 1d4a1a3563d148e0008fb76ba6047210c573172e we added `WithAdmissionControl()` variants of these that allow the caller to specify the admission control source and priority, so one option here is to simply migrate all callers. However, there are hundreds of call sites so this will be spotty and annoying.

It would probably be better if we could set the source and priority for an entire component, such that all derived requests get a proper admission control header. One way to do this would be to set it in the context. We should likely also subject all requests to admission control by default, and instead allow callers to explicitly opt out, e.g. by marking themselves as system traffic.

Test tooling to assert correct admission control headers would also be useful.

Jira issue: CRDB-14639
cockroachdb/cockroach#79212.title: admission: subject internal traffic to admission control

cockroachdb/cockroach#101715.body:
**Describe the problem**

In PostgreSQL, `SHOW ALL` returns three columns. From https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-show.html:

```
SHOW ALL;

            name         | setting |                description
-------------------------+---------+-------------------------------------------------
 allow_system_table_mods | off     | Allows modifications of the structure of ...
```

In CockroachDB, it returns two columns. From https://www.cockroachlabs.com/docs/stable/show-vars.html#showing-the-value-of-all-session-variables:

```
SHOW ALL;

       variable       |      value
----------------------+-------------------
  application_name    | $ cockroach demo
```

Description is missing.

That's the first thing that users of FerretDB notice when they want to try to run it with CockroachDB. See https://github.com/FerretDB/FerretDB/discussions/2462#discussioncomment-5645177

Jira issue: CRDB-27099


Epic CRDB-49582
cockroachdb/cockroach#101715.title: sql: `SHOW ALL` returns two columns instead of three

cockroachdb/cockroach#156800.body:
When an export request encounters intents, it can lead to large volumes of intent resolution done on behalf of the export. Depending on the state of Raft and AC, this work can be slow, leading to backups timing out. 

Export request can resolve the abandoned intents of finalized (committed or aborted) transactions, but because export [can run with high priority](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/bc1b6d6cc313d6fcb909e5e473fddb5ed67722d2/pkg/backup/backup_processor.go#L528), it can also try to push and rewrite the intents of long-running write transactions.

We already have a test (`TestCleanupIntentsDuringBackupPerformanceRegression`) that exercises the abandoned intents case by ensuring the backup doesn't fail and by asserting on the expected number of intent resolution batches. 

As part of validating the virtualized intent resolution work, it would be helpful to extend this test to a roachtest and measure the actual latency of export requests, as well as the effect on the writers. This test can either assert on various latencies, or export them to roachperf.


Jira issue: CRDB-56198

Epic CRDB-49120
cockroachdb/cockroach#156800.title: roachtest: add test for export request with contention

Following up the Canary Statistics Rollout idea introduced in https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/issues/150015, we should add clearer observability of this feature, since this feature bring more complicated states for statistics, and it might cause confusion and hard to debug. 

Listing ideas for enhancement:

- [x] Dedicated sql statement statistics for query invocation that uses the canary path. (Frontend: #https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/issues/165250)
- [x] Dedicated cluster metrics for query latency with the canary path.
- [x] Session variable `stats_as_of=<timestamp>` and `stats_canary={true|false}` to enable planning a query retroactively with not-most-recent statistics. 
- [x] Showing if canary stats is used in a plan in the output of `EXPLAIN`.

cockroachdb/cockroach#157897.body:
Split evaluations generate a batch which is eventually applied to storage on all replicas (if committed). This batch contains storage updates that materialize the new RHS (right-hand-side) range, and change some metadata of both LHS/RHS.

By design, we want this batch to touch only replicated / state machine keys (so that it can be written to the state machine engine). Today, it mostly does, with one exception: it writes [LastReplicaGCTimestamp](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/82a0890ea98e1d52874a114c0ebf78a3afc4f3b1/pkg/kv/kvserver/batcheval/cmd_end_transaction.go#L1360-L1365) for the RHS. This is a "log engine" / unreplicated key which does not make sense outside of a local replica (it's used by replica GC queue on each node). It is marked with `spanset.DisableForbiddenSpanAssertions` accordingly as a workaround.

We want to stop writing this key at the evaluation end, and instead write it locally at the split [apply](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/939076d551dacb52d2afd0ab8e157d72c264c22f/pkg/kv/kvserver/store_split.go#L120) side. TBD how the initial timestamp is determined for the RHS replica, one of: (a) copy from the local LHS replica, (b) set to 0, (c) set to `now`, or something else.

Backwards compatibility needs to be considered: we can have historical proposals with this key populated. A workaround could be similar to one for the other user of `spanset.DisableForbiddenSpanAssertions` (the `RaftTruncatedState` key) made recently:

https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/82a0890ea98e1d52874a114c0ebf78a3afc4f3b1/pkg/kv/kvserver/store_split.go#L124-L146

Epic: CRDB-55220
Jira issue: CRDB-57004
cockroachdb/cockroach#157897.title: batcheval: do not write LastReplicaGCTimestamp in splits

cockroachdb/cockroach#160004.body:
**Describe the problem**
When generating an encryption key using `cockroach gen encryption-key --size 256 --version 2`, the generated JWK incorrectly contains `"alg": "cockroach-aes-192-ctr-v2"` instead of the expected `"cockroach-aes-256-ctr-v2"`.

**To Reproduce**
1. Run `cockroach gen encryption-key --size 256 --version 2 out.key`
2. Inspect `out.key`:
   ```json
   {"keys":[{"alg":"cockroach-aes-192-ctr-v2","k":"...","kid":"...","kty":"oct"}]}
   ```
3. Note that the key `k` is 32 bytes (256 bits) after base64 decoding, but the `alg` field specifies 192 bits.

**Expected behavior**
The `alg` field should match the specified size: `"alg": "cockroach-aes-256-ctr-v2"`.

**Root Cause**
There is a typo in `pkg/storage/enginepb/key_registry.go` within the `JWKAlgorithm()` method:
```go
	case EncryptionType_AES_256_CTR_V2:
		return "cockroach-aes-192-ctr-v2", nil
```
It should return `"cockroach-aes-256-ctr-v2"`.

**Impact**
While the underlying Go `aes` package will use AES-256 because the key is 32 bytes, internal CockroachDB logic that relies on the `EncryptionType` enum (derived from the `alg` string) will incorrectly treat it as a 192-bit key. This might lead to 192-bit data keys being generated even when a 256-bit master key is used.

Jira issue: CRDB-58116
cockroachdb/cockroach#160004.title: cli: gen encryption-key --size 256 --version 2 produces incorrect "alg" string

cockroachdb/cockroach#165339.body: The `schemachange/bulkingest/nodes=12/rows=4000000000/elastic=false/add_column` roachtest variant shows that disabling elastic CPU control does not improve index backfill throughput compared to the default (elastic=true). **Expected behavior:** The `elastic=false` variant disables both `kvadmission.elastic_control_bulk_low_priority.enabled` and `bulkio.index_backfill.elastic_control.enabled` ([schemachange.go:648-651](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/master/pkg/cmd/roachtest/tests/schemachange.go#L648)). Without elastic CPU throttling, the index backfill should be able to use more CPU and achieve higher throughput. **Observed behavior (from [roachperf](https://roachperf.crdb.dev/?filter=schemachange%2Fbulkingest)):** `elastic=false/add_column`: ~60,000–78,000 rows/s/node (stable, ~1 month of data): Default `add_column` (elastic=true): historically ~100,000–110,000 rows/s/node, recently ~70,000–90,000: The `elastic=false` variant is consistently **slower** than the default, which is the opposite of what we'd expect. Disabling elastic control should remove throttling and increase throughput, not decrease it. **Investigation questions:** 1. Are the two cluster settings actually taking effect? Verify in the test logs that the settings are applied before the backfill starts. 2. Is there a different bottleneck (disk I/O, network, compaction) that becomes the limiting factor when elastic CPU throttling is removed, causing the system to overload and actually perform worse? 3. Is the elastic CPU control interacting with admission control in a way that actually improves throughput by pacing work and avoiding contention? 4. Are the two test variants running on comparable hardware? Check if there are systematic differences in the cloud instance types or disk configurations.
cockroachdb/cockroach#165339.title: bulkingest: elastic=false does not improve index backfill throughput

cockroachdb/cockroach#165387.body:
The Kafka v2 sink contains logic to split up a large batch to avoid choking on a MessageTooLarge: https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/27a25fc5361acf8027aab6f5ee227fff28611dfd/pkg/ccl/changefeedccl/sink_kafka_v2.go#L158.

There is a problem with this logic: when multiple parallel batches are submitted, they queue inside franz-go and get coalesced into per-broker requests. If that request ends up being too large, every request in the batch fails with MessageTooLarge.

This means we sometimes see MessageTooLarge in a single message batch that could fit in its own batch, but it happened to get bundled with other messages. This is an issue because when this occurs, we return the error instead of retrying it: https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/27a25fc5361acf8027aab6f5ee227fff28611dfd/pkg/ccl/changefeedccl/sink_kafka_v2.go#L180. This causes the flow to restart with MessageTooLarge.

There are a few factors that increase the probability of observing this:
1. Rows that close to the max message size or batches that are configured to be close to the broker's message size limit.
2. By default, we set the sink concurrency to the number of cores. The higher the concurrency, the more batches can get coalesced into a single broker flush and trigger this error. 

The reason we run into this is we hard code the max producer max batch size as [256 MiB](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/27a25fc5361acf8027aab6f5ee227fff28611dfd/pkg/ccl/changefeedccl/sink_kafka_v2.go#L85). The intent of this change is to avoid spurious client errors, but it also means that franz-go will form massive batches internally.

I confirmed that setting ProducerBatchMaxBytes to 1 MiB fixes the spurious errors, but we will need to find a different fix because customers control the maximum value accepted by the kafka brokers. 

Jira issue: CRDB-61362
cockroachdb/cockroach#165387.title: changefeed: kafka v2 sink can observe spurious large message errors

This is easily reproducible on a fresh `roachprod` cluster. See [reproduction doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/14QbJZR0oaF1KYpkHJSASgARWnXiNKIKZR49fW1ixV5w/edit?tab=t.0).

                     |               |             |            |             |                     |                            |     "actual": 1800009482,
                     |               |             |            |             |                     |                            |     "expected": 900004741

cockroachdb/cockroach#165548.body:
**Describe the problem:**

In the legacy schema changer, when a `CREATE TABLE` statement with inline `FOREIGN KEY` constraints is rolled back (e.g. because the schema change job fails permanently), the rollback path marks the new table as `Dropped` and queues it for GC, but **does not remove the `InboundFK` entries** that were added to the referenced table(s) during the original SQL transaction.

This leaves orphaned FK back-references pointing to a descriptor that no longer exists, causing descriptor validation errors such as:

```
relation "parent" (209): invalid foreign key backreference:
missing table=264: referenced descriptor not found
```

The bug is in the ADD-state rollback path of `rollbackSchemaChange()`. View dependency cleanup already exists in that path, but FK back-reference cleanup was missing entirely.

**To Reproduce:**

1. Create a parent table:
   ```sql
   CREATE TABLE parent (id INT PRIMARY KEY);
   ```
2. Begin a `CREATE TABLE` with an inline `FOREIGN KEY` referencing `parent`, in a way that causes the schema change job to fail permanently (e.g. inject a job failure, or construct a scenario where the job cannot complete):
   ```sql
   CREATE TABLE child (
     id INT PRIMARY KEY,
     parent_id INT REFERENCES parent(id)
   );
   ```
3. Allow the schema change job to fail and roll back.
4. Inspect the `parent` table descriptor. It retains an `InboundFK` entry pointing to the now-dropped `child` table.
5. Run descriptor validation — observe the error:
   ```
   relation "parent" (209): invalid foreign key backreference:
   missing table=264: referenced descriptor not found
   ```

**Expected behavior:**

When the `CREATE TABLE` schema change is rolled back, all FK back-references added to referenced tables (`InboundFK`s) and any outbound FK entries on tables referencing the rolled-back table (`OutboundFK`s) should be cleaned up. After rollback, referenced table descriptors should be left in a valid state with no dangling references.

**Environment:**

- CockroachDB version: v25.1 and later
- Schema changer: Legacy schema changer
- Deployment: Any

**Additional context:**

The fix reuses the existing `removeFKBackReferenceFromTable()` helper (already used by `dropTableImpl()` and `maybeReverseMutations()`) in the ADD-state rollback path of `rollbackSchemaChange()`, iterating over both outbound and inbound FKs on the table being rolled back. Cleanup errors are logged as warnings and skipped (best-effort), matching the pattern used by `dropViewDeps()`.

Fixed in commit [`b49a6a2`](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/commit/b49a6a299bed65e68cd183ff151f4fa59dc6d2ad).

Jira issue: CRDB-61445

The data and metadata of the ingested sstable will never be synced.
See detailed analysis in https://docs.google.com/document/d/1G0zlJ0mwdiYEgvdB7FhDXunSOiKqCANh5Z6I4l0YEzo/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.upwu7f287li7, created due to the discussions in https://cockroachlabs.slack.com/archives/CAC6K3SLU/p1773252199032959 and https://cockroachlabs.slack.com/archives/CAC6K3SLU/p1773159123755159?thread_ts=1773083591.589999&cid=CAC6K3SLU.

It is unclear whether we need this cluster setting. There are some past suggestions to increase the value from the default of 512KiB, so there seems to be some benefit of keeping it.

cockroachdb/cockroach#165984.body:
## Summary

`CREATE TEMP TABLE` fails with a privilege error if the user lacks `CREATE` on the `public` schema, even though temp tables are created in the session's `pg_temp` schema. PostgreSQL allows this.

Additionally, CockroachDB is missing support for the `TEMPORARY` database-level privilege that PostgreSQL uses to control temp table creation.

## To Reproduce

```sql
-- As root:
REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM PUBLIC;
SET CLUSTER SETTING sql.auth.public_schema_create_privilege.enabled=false;
CREATE USER temp;

-- As temp:
SET experimental_enable_temp_tables = 'on';
CREATE TEMP TABLE users (id UUID PRIMARY KEY);
-- ERROR: user temp does not have CREATE privilege on schema public
```

## Expected behavior

The `CREATE TEMP TABLE` should succeed, matching PostgreSQL behavior. PostgreSQL does not require `CREATE` on `public` to create temporary tables — they go into `pg_temp_*`.

Verified on PostgreSQL 14:
```sql
-- As a user with no CREATE on public:
CREATE TEMP TABLE users2 (id UUID PRIMARY KEY);
-- CREATE TABLE (succeeds)
SELECT schemaname, tablename FROM pg_tables WHERE tablename = 'users2';
--  pg_temp_39 | users2
```

## Missing `TEMPORARY` database privilege

In PostgreSQL, temp table creation is controlled by a **`TEMPORARY`** (aka `TEMP`) database-level privilege, not by any schema-level `CREATE` privilege. Key details:

- `TEMPORARY` is granted to `PUBLIC` by default, so all users can create temp tables unless explicitly revoked.
- `GRANT/REVOKE TEMPORARY ON DATABASE dbname TO/FROM role` controls it.
- When revoked, PostgreSQL returns: `permission denied to create temporary tables in database "postgres"`

```sql
-- PostgreSQL behavior:
REVOKE TEMPORARY ON DATABASE postgres FROM PUBLIC;

-- As any non-superuser:
CREATE TEMP TABLE t (id int);
-- ERROR: permission denied to create temporary tables in database "postgres"

GRANT TEMPORARY ON DATABASE postgres TO PUBLIC;
-- Now temp tables work again, regardless of schema-level CREATE privileges.
```

CockroachDB does not implement the `TEMPORARY` database privilege at all. This means there is no way to explicitly control temp table creation per-user/per-database as PostgreSQL allows.

## Root Cause

In the optimizer's `buildCreateTable` ([create_table.go:22-37](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/master/pkg/sql/opt/optbuilder/create_table.go#L22-L37)), when a table is temporary:
1. The schema name is cleared (`SchemaName = ""`, `ExplicitSchema = false`) since temp tables go into `pg_temp`.
2. `resolveSchemaForCreateTable` is called, which resolves the now-unqualified name to `public` via the search path.
3. `resolveSchemaForCreate` in [util.go:513](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/master/pkg/sql/opt/optbuilder/util.go#L513) then checks `CREATE` privilege on the resolved schema (`public`), which fails.

At execution time, `getSchemaForCreateTable` ([create_table.go:130-152](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/master/pkg/sql/create_table.go#L130-L152)) correctly resolves to the temporary schema, and `canCreateOnSchema` ([authorization.go:813-815](https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/master/pkg/sql/authorization.go#L813-L815)) correctly skips the privilege check for `SchemaTemporary`. But the optimizer-level check blocks execution before it gets there.

## Suggested Fix

1. **Bug fix (immediate):** Skip the `CREATE` privilege check in `resolveSchemaForCreate` when the table persistence is temporary, since the resolved schema is just a placeholder and the real schema (pg_temp) is determined at execution time.
2. **Feature (follow-up):** Implement the `TEMPORARY` database privilege to match PostgreSQL's privilege model for controlling temp table creation.

Jira issue: CRDB-61706

Epic CRDB-17128

cockroachdb/cockroach#166361.body:
**Describe the problem**

It's possible to create a duplicate function (same name, same signature) that can then not be dropped.

**To Reproduce**

Create a non-public schema, create two functions in that schema, then rename the second to match the first.

```
CREATE SCHEMA myschema;

CREATE FUNCTION myschema.fa(a INT) RETURNS INT AS 'SELECT a' LANGUAGE SQL;
CREATE FUNCTION myschema.fb(a INT) RETURNS INT AS 'SELECT a' LANGUAGE SQL;

ALTER FUNCTION myschema.fb RENAME TO fa;

DROP FUNCTION myschema.fa;
```

The final `DROP FUNCTION` returns this message:

```
ERROR: function name "fa" is not unique
SQLSTATE: 42725
```

**Expected behavior**
I suspect the 'public' schema is the only schema being checked instead of the `myschema` schema during the `RENAME`. The expected behaviour would be that the rename would fail because there already exists a function with a matching name+signature.

**Environment:**
 - CockroachDB version: v26.2.0 (master)

**Additional context**
Not possible to drop either function.

Jira issue: CRDB-61872

Epic CRDB-60817

cockroachdb/cockroach#166456.body:
**Describe the problem**

In shared-process multi-tenant mode, the ASH (Active Session History) virtual tables
`crdb_internal.node_active_session_history` and `crdb_internal.cluster_active_session_history`
do not enforce tenant isolation. A secondary tenant can see ASH samples belonging to other
tenants and the system tenant.

The root cause is that the virtual table populate functions in `pkg/sql/crdb_internal.go`
call `ListLocalActiveSessionHistory` / `ListActiveSessionHistory` without setting the
tenant context on the `ctx`. Those RPC handlers rely on `roachpb.ClientTenantFromContext(ctx)`
to determine which tenant is querying and filter samples accordingly. That context value
is normally set by the gRPC authentication interceptor in `pkg/rpc/auth.go`, but in
shared-process mode the call is in-process (not gRPC), so the interceptor never runs and
`ClientTenantFromContext` returns `ok=false`. This makes `filterByTenant = false`,
causing the handler to return all samples unfiltered.

**To Reproduce**

1. Start a CockroachDB server with `DefaultTestTenant: base.TestControlsTenantsExplicitly`
2. Enable ASH: `SET CLUSTER SETTING obs.ash.enabled = true`
3. Start two shared-process tenants (e.g. tenant IDs 10 and 11)
4. Generate work on both tenants with distinct `application_name` values
5. From tenant 10, query `SELECT * FROM crdb_internal.node_active_session_history`
6. Observe that samples with `tenant_id = 11` (and the other tenant's `app_name`) are visible

**Expected behavior**

Each secondary tenant should only see ASH samples matching its own `tenant_id`. The system
tenant should see all samples. This is the documented behavior in the code comments on
`ListLocalActiveSessionHistory`.

**Additional data / screenshots**

The fix is to propagate the planner's tenant ID into the context before calling the handler:

```go
// pkg/sql/crdb_internal.go — node_active_session_history populate function
ashCtx := roachpb.ContextWithClientTenant(ctx, p.extendedEvalCtx.Codec.TenantID)
response, err := p.extendedEvalCtx.SQLStatusServer.ListLocalActiveSessionHistory(
    ashCtx, &serverpb.ListActiveSessionHistoryRequest{},
)
```

The same fix is needed for the `cluster_active_session_history` populate function.

**Environment:**
 - CockroachDB version: master (post-ASH merge)
 - Affects: shared-process multi-tenant deployments only

**Additional context**

This does not affect separate-process tenants (each has its own sampler and ring buffer).
It only affects shared-process tenants where all tenants share a single process-wide
ASH ring buffer and rely on `ListLocalActiveSessionHistory` for tenant filtering.

No other virtual table is affected because ASH is the only one that uses
`ClientTenantFromContext` for tenant-level sample filtering in the RPC handler. Other
tables (sessions, contention events) either don't have tenant metadata or filter by
user identity instead.

Jira issue: CRDB-61923
cockroachdb/cockroach#166456.title: obs/ash: tenant isolation broken for ASH virtual tables in shared-process mode

cockroachdb/pebble#5846.body: Blob files seem to be getting rewritten despite not hitting the maximum blob reference depth. In the following cluster, value separation was enabled at 18:13 and it was ~18:41 when the compaction bandwidth finally dropped, causing an increase in SQL throughput. This was probably because even 1 input sstable in a compaction that was written pre-value separation causes blob file rewrites, due to https://github.com/cockroachdb/pebble/blob/d304d514d0d43cc706055fec89ef540865f60729/compaction_value_separation.go#L156 (`backingProps.ValueSeparationMinSize` will be 0). Jira issue: PEBBLE-1370
cockroachdb/pebble#5846.title: db: enabling value separation continues to aggressively rewrite blob files
